setting.note: Between London and a Paris sliding into revolution, Dickens weaves the fates of the recalled prisoner Dr Manette, his daughter Lucie, the aristocrat Charles Darnay and the wasted lawyer Sydney Carton, toward a sacrifice at the guillotine. The novel maps two real cities: Tellson's Bank and the Old Bailey in London, and in Paris the wine-shop of Saint-Antoine, the stormed Bastille, and the Place de la Révolution where the blade falls.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Charles Dickens (d. 1870), first published 1859. Mapped as a cited work: we store no text and reproduce no passages here. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. This entry reproduces no text; the public-domain original is widely available.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. The original text is in the public domain.
rights.attribution: A Tale of Two Cities, Charles Dickens (1859). Locations mapped by Read the Places.

palette.note: The red of the spilled wine that stains Saint-Antoine and the blood it foretells, the colour of a revolution turning from justice to terror. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
